title: Daily health check for meat rabbits
subtitle: The two-minute check that catches problems before they become losses.
excerpt: Rabbits hide illness until they cannot. By the time a rabbit looks obviously unwell, the problem has usually been developing for a day or two. A quick daily check against a short list of signs catches issues early enough to act on.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Rabbits (2009, Crown copyright). https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/animal-welfare-codes-of-practice
Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (Sasquatch Books, 12th ed. 2012), rabbit health chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soft, grape-cluster dropping produced by rabbits in the early morning and re-eaten directly from the anus. Rich in B vitamins and protein. A rabbit that stops eating caecotropes develops deficiencies quickly; finding them in the bedding rather than being consumed is a health flag.

The muscular contractions that move food through the digestive tract. In rabbits, gut motility is very sensitive to stress, diet change, and pain; it slows or stops in unwell animals, producing the life-threatening condition GI stasis.

content.text: A daily check for each rabbit takes two minutes per animal. The routine is always the same: observe from outside the cage first, then open and handle the animal briefly. Consistency matters because you are looking for changes from the baseline of what you already know.
Observe before you open the cage

Stand at the cage for ten seconds. A healthy rabbit at rest is alert, its ears upright, and it acknowledges your presence. It may stretch, groom, or move to the feeding station. A rabbit crouched in a corner with its coat rough and its eyes half-closed is telling you something is wrong. Note whether it moved during your approach.

Eyes should be clear and bright. A discharge, whether clear, cloudy, or crusty, is worth watching. Snuffles is a common rabbit condition: a runny nose and sneezing that may be an infection or an allergy. It is rarely an emergency but spreads between animals. Isolate any rabbit with a runny nose until you can assess whether it is improving.
Normal hard droppings are round, firm, and dry. Very small or very few droppings, or none at all, means
is slowing. Soft, runny, or mucous droppings suggest a digestive problem.
found lying unconsumed in the bedding are a sign the rabbit is unwell.

Run your hand along the belly. It should feel soft and yielding. A hard, drum-tight abdomen indicates bloat. A rabbit with bloat sits in a hunched posture and does not eat; this is a vet call the same day.

Check the area around the vent and the base of the tail. Any soiling with wet droppings is a flystrike risk in warm weather. Flies lay eggs on soiled fur within hours; maggots can be fatal within a day. A rabbit with a soiled rear needs cleaning immediately and a thorough check of the surrounding skin. This check is especially important from May to September.

Check the hind pads for sore hocks, a pressure sore on the back feet common in rabbits kept on wire floors. A red, raw, or ulcerated pad needs straw or a solid resting board added immediately. Nails should be trimmed once every six to eight weeks to prevent curl-under that makes movement painful.

attrs.title: Food and water intake as the baseline
attrs.body: The first thing a sick rabbit does is stop eating. If a rabbit does not come to the feeder during your morning visit and was eating normally the day before, move straight to the five-point check. A rabbit that has not eaten for 12 hours and is not passing droppings needs the same-day attention; 24 hours without gut motility is a medical emergency.

items.symptom: Rabbit has not eaten but is still alert and moving
items.cause: Diet change, stress from a predator at night, or early-stage digestive upset
items.fix: Offer fresh hay. Remove any recent additions to the diet. Watch at the next feeding. A rabbit that is alert but not eating often resolves in a few hours; one that is hunched and still not eating at the evening check needs closer assessment.

items.symptom: Head tilting to one side, loss of balance
items.cause: E. cuniculi, a common rabbit parasite affecting the brain and kidneys, or inner-ear infection
items.fix: Isolate immediately and call the vet. Head tilt has several causes; the prognosis depends on rapid diagnosis. Do not put off the call.

items.symptom: Rabbit found dead with no warning signs at the previous check
items.cause: GI stasis or bloat progressing very rapidly; heart condition; toxic plant if any access to forage
items.fix: Record the date and circumstances. Review what the animal was eating in the previous 48 hours. If other animals in the same colony begin to show similar signs, call the vet; a colony-level disease event needs professional input.
